text: Graphs Chapter 20 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Terminology • In the context of this book, graphs represent relations among data items • G = { V, E} – A graph is a set of vertices (nodes) and – A set of edges that connect the vertices © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved FIGURE 20-1 An ordinary line graph Terminology FIGURE 20-2 A graph and one of its subgraphs © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Two vertices of a graph are adjacent if they are joined by an edge • A path between two vertices is a sequence of edges that begins at one vertex and ends at another vertex Terminology FIGURE 20-3 Examples of graphs that are either connected, disconnected, or complete © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • A graph is connected if each pair of distinct vertices has a path between them • In a complete graph, each pair of distinct vertices has an edge between them • A cycle is a path that begins and ends at the same vertex • A simple cycle is a cycle that does not pass through other vertices more than once Terminology FIGURE 20-4 Graph-like structures that are not graphs © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Because a graph has a set of edges, a graph cannot have duplicate edges between vertices • A multigraph does allow multiple edges; thus, a multigraph is not a graph • A graph’s edges cannot begin and end at the same vertex, a.k.a. self edge or loop Terminology FIGURE 20-5 (a) A weighted graph © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved A weighted graph has a set of edges with numeric values. Terminology FIGURE 20-5 Examples of two kinds of graphs © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Each edge in a directed graph, or digraph, has a direction and is called a directed edge • A directed graph can have two edges between a pair of vertices, one in each direction • A directed path is a sequence of directed edges between two vertices, e.g., there is a directed path that begins in Providence, goes to New York, and ends in San Francisco • If there is a directed edge from vertex x to vertex y, then y is adjacent to x. • Albuquerque is adjacent to San Francisco, but San Francisco is not adjacent to Albuquerque • Indegree – number of edges going into a node • Outdegree – number of edges coming out of a node • What is the indegree of New York? • 3 • What is the outdegree of San Francisco? • 2 Graphs as ADTs ADT graph operations – Test if empty – Get number of vertices, edges in a graph – See if edge exists between two given vertices – Add vertex to graph whose vertices have distinct, different values from new vertex – Add/remove edge between two given vertices – Remove vertex, edges to other vertices – Retrieve vertex that contains given value – Traverse the graph © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Graphs as ADTs LISTING 20-1 A C++ interface for undirected, connected graphs © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Implementing Graphs FIGURE 20-7 (a) A directed graph and (b) its adjacency matrix © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved An adjacency matrix for a graph with n vertices numbered 0, 1, . . ., n – 1 is an n by n array matrix such that matrix[i][j] is 1 (true) if there is an edge from vertex i to vertex j, and 0 (false) otherwise. Implementing Graphs FIGURE 20-8 (a) A weighted undirected graph and (b) its adjacency matrix © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Implementing Graphs FIGURE 20-9 (a) A directed graph and (b) its adjacency list © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Implementing Graphs FIGURE 20-10 (a) A weighted undirected graph and (b) its adjacency list © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Implementing Graphs • Adjacency list – Often requires less space than adjacency matrix – Supports finding vertices adjacent to given vertex by traversing the ith linked chain to determine whether a vertex corresponding to vertex j is present • Adjacency matrix – Supports process of seeing if there exists an edge from vertex i to vertex j © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Graph Traversals • Visits all vertices it can reach • Visits all vertices if and only if the graph is connected, regardless where the traversal starts • Can be used to determine if graph is connected • Mark/check that a vertex is never visited more than once, to prevent infinite loop, in case of a cycle © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Depth-First Search • DFS traversal – Goes as far as possible from a vertex before backing up © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Depth-First Search • Recursive transversal algorithm © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Breadth-First Search • BFS traversal – Visits all vertices adjacent to a vertex before going forward © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Depth-First Search The results of a depth- first traversal, beginning at vertex a. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Breadth-First Search The results of a breadth- first traversal, beginning at vertex a. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Applications of Graphs • Topological Sorting • Spanning Trees • Minimum Spanning Trees • Shortest Paths • Circuits • Some Difficult Problems © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Topological Sorting FIGURE 20-15 A directed graph without cycles © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • A topological order is a linear order of the vertices in a directed graph without cycles. • All of the edges point in one direction, if you arrange the vertices of digraph linearly and in topological order. • Arranging the vertices into a topological order is called topological sorting. Spanning Trees • A tree is an undirected connected graph without cycles • The nodes (vertices) of a tree have a hierarchical arrangement that is not required of all graphs • Detecting a cycle in an undirected graph – Connected undirected graph with n vertices must have at least n – 1 edges – If it has exactly n – 1 edges, it cannot contain a cycle – With more than n – 1 edges, must contain at least one cycle © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Spanning Trees FIGURE 20-19 A spanning tree for the above graph © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved The dashed lines in Figure 20-19 indicate edges that were omitted from the graph to form the tree. • There may be several spanning trees for a given graph. • If you have a connected undirected graph with cycles and you remove edges until there are no cycles, you will obtain a spanning tree for the graph. Spanning Trees FIGURE 20-20 Connected graphs that each have four vertices and three edges © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Spanning Trees DFS spanning tree algorithm © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Spanning Trees FIGURE 20-21 The DFS spanning tree rooted at vertex a © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Spanning Trees BFS spanning tree algorithm © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Spanning Trees FIGURE 20-23 The BFS spanning tree rooted at vertex a © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Minimum Spanning Trees FIGURE 20-24 A weighted, connected, undirected graph © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • A minimum spanning tree of a connected undirected graph has a minimal edge-weight sum. • A minimum spanning tree need not be unique, since there may be several minimum spanning trees for a particular graph, where their costs are equal. Minimum Spanning Trees Prims algorithm © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Prim's algorithm is a greedy algorithm that finds a minimum spanning tree for a weighted undirected graph. (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prim%27s_algorithm) 1. Initialize a tree with a single vertex, chosen arbitrarily from the graph. 2. Grow the tree by one edge: Of the edges that connect the tree to vertices not yet in the tree, find the minimum-weight edge, and transfer it to the tree. 3. Repeat step 2 (until all vertices are in the tree). Minimum Spanning Trees FIGURE 20-25 A trace of primsAlgorithm for the graph in Figure 20-23, beginning at vertex a © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Shortest Paths • The shortest path between two vertices in a weighted graph – Has the smallest edge-weight sum © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved FIGURE 20-26 (a) A weighted directed graph and (b) its adjacency matrix Shortest Paths FIGURE 20-27 A trace of the shortest-path (Dijkstra) algorithm applied to the graph in Figure 20-26a © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Dijkstra's algorithm(1956, 1959) finds the shortest path from a given source node to every other node. Shortest Paths FIGURE 20-28 Checking weight[u] by examining the graph: (a) weight[2] in step 2; (b) weight[1] in step 3; © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Shortest Paths FIGURE 20-28 Checking weight[u] by examining the graph: (c) weight[3] in step 3; (d) weight[3] in step 4 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Circuits • Circuit – Another name for type of cycle common in statement of certain types of problems – Typical circuits either visit every vertex once or every edge once • Euler Circuit – Begins at vertex v – Passes through every edge exactly once – Terminates at v © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Circuits FIGURE 20-28 (a) Euler’s bridge problem and (b) its multigraph representation © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • A path in an undirected graph that begins at a vertex v, passes through every edge in the graph exactly once, and terminates at v is called an Euler circuit. • An Euler circuit exists if and only if each vertex touches an even number of edges. Euler demonstrated that no solution exists for this particular configuration of edges and vertices. Circuits FIGURE 20-29 Pencil and paper drawings © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Finding an Euler circuit is like drawing each of the diagrams in Figure 20-29 (a) without lifting your pencil or redrawing a line, and ending at your starting point. You can find one for Figure 20-29 (b). No solution is possible for Figure 20-29 (a). Some Difficult Problems • Hamilton circuit – Begins at vertex v – Passes through every vertex exactly once – Terminates at v • Variation is “traveling salesperson problem” – Visit every city on his route exactly once – Edge (road) has associated cost (mileage) – Goal is determine least expensive circuit © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Traveling Salesperson Problem • Famous problem which has not been solved efficiently. • There are numerous algorithms – Brute Force Algorithm O(n!) • generate all possible city permutations and calculate each route's length – Nearest Neighbor Algorithm O(n2) • starts at a random city and repeatedly visits the nearest city until all have been visited • Approximation – Bellman-Held-Karp algorithm O(2n * n2) • Fastest known algorithm for solving general problem © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved https://www.networkpages.nl/a-big-breakthrough-in-the-euclidean-travelling-salesman- problem/#:~:text=For%20the%20Travelling%20Salesman%20Problem,to%20come%20to%20an%20answer. Traveling Salesperson Problem • Bellman-Held-Karp algorithm O(2n * n2) • Assume n = 100 cities • Perform roughly 2n * n2 steps to come to an answer. • It takes computer roughly 2100 *10,000=2 * 1033 (a number with 33 digits) steps to give an answer using this algorithm. • Consider you have a 2GHz processor, then this performs 2 * 109 operations per second. • This processor needs 1024 seconds to give an answer to this problem. • How many years are there in 1024 seconds? – ~3.17×1016 years – The age of the universe is estimated to be 13.8 billion years, or ~ 1.38×1010 years. • Companies use algorithms that can solve specific instances of TSP very fast. • They don’t solve the general problem but they use the structure of a specific case to solve it fast. • In practice these algorithms give solutions that are optimal or almost optimal, but that is not guaranteed to be the case. • Fastest known algorithm for solving general problem © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved https://www.networkpages.nl/a-big-breakthrough-in-the-euclidean-travelling-salesman- problem/#:~:text=For%20the%20Travelling%20Salesman%20Problem,to%20come%20to%20an%20answer.
